subject: Letter from the Don
excerpt: The Russian winter has arrived. We have equipment for autumn, not this. But we are Italians — we improvise, we survive...
context: By autumn 1942, the Italian 8th Army (ARMIR) held a 270-km sector of the Don River front as part of the Axis forces supporting the German thrust toward Stalingrad. The Italian forces were critically underequipped for the Soviet winter, lacking adequate winter clothing, anti-tank weapons, and artillery. The Soviet Operation Ostrogozhsk-Rossosh in January 1943 would shatter the 8th Army entirely.
original_text:
Cara Mamma,

L'inverno russo è arrivato ed è al di là di qualsiasi cosa avessi immaginato. Abbiamo equipaggiamento per l'autunno, non per questo freddo. I tedeschi sono meglio attrezzati, ma noi ce la caviamo. Siamo italiani — improvvisiamo, sopravviviamo, condividiamo quel poco che abbiamo.

Il paese qui è piatto oltre ogni credere. La steppa si estende all'orizzonte in ogni direzione — nessuna collina, nessun albero, solo cielo ed erba gelata. È bella in modo terribile.

I pacchi alimentari che hai mandato sono arrivati danneggiati ma ugualmente accolti con gratitudine. Il vino soprattutto — condiviso con tutta la mia sezione nella festa di San Martino, un piccolo calore in questo posto gelato.

Prega per me. E per tutti noi qui.

Il tuo Lorenzo
translation:
Dear Mother,

The Russian winter has arrived and it is beyond anything I had imagined. We have equipment for autumn, not for this cold. The Germans are better equipped, but we manage. We are Italians — we improvise, we survive, we share what little we have.

The country here is flat beyond belief. The steppe extends to the horizon in every direction — no hills, no trees, just sky and frozen grass. It is beautiful in a terrible way.

The food packages you sent arrived damaged but still gratefully received. The wine most of all — shared with my whole section on the feast of San Martino, a small warmth in this frozen place.

Pray for me. And for all of us here.

Your Lorenzo
notes: Ferretti was among the survivors of the destruction of the 8th Army during Operation Ostrogozhsk-Rossosh in January 1943. He walked over 600 km west in the winter retreat before reaching German lines.

sources.ref: Original letter, Archivio di Stato di Roma, Fondo ARMIR, b. 14.
sources.note: Italian-language original; translation by VeteransLedger editorial staff.

sources.ref: Schlemmer, Thomas. Invasori, non vittime: La campagna italiana di Russia 1941–1943. Laterza, 2009.
Revelli, Nuto. La guerra dei poveri. Einaudi, 1962. (Collected testimonies of Italian soldiers on the Eastern Front.)

subject: From the desert — before the battle
excerpt: The desert here is magnificent in the early morning, before the heat begins. Rommel is nearby — one sees his staff cars passing. We wait...
context: The Ariete Armoured Division was one of the most effective Italian formations in North Africa. By September 1942 the Axis forces had been halted at El Alamein after the failure of the First Battle of El Alamein. Both sides were preparing for the decisive engagement; the British launched Operation Lightfoot on 23 October 1942. The Ariete Division fought to virtual annihilation holding the southern flank at Alamein, earning respect from Rommel himself.
original_text:
Carissima Sofia,

Ti scrivo dal deserto, che confesso di aver imparato ad amare in modo strano. Al mattino presto, prima che cominci il caldo, è magnifico — la luce sulla sabbia, il silenzio, il lontano azzurro del mare. Poi arriva mezzogiorno e si preferirebbe essere ovunque altro.

Il quartier generale di Rommel non è lontano — si vedono le sue auto dello stato maggiore sulla strada costiera, che lasciano polvere. Gli uomini lo rispettano, anche se è tedesco. Combatte fianco a fianco con i suoi uomini.

Ci hanno detto che un'offensiva britannica sta arrivando. Saremo pronti. La Divisione Ariete non si è mai ritirata quando poteva avanzare, e non intendiamo cominciare adesso.

Penso a te e ai bambini ogni sera. Tieni la fotografia che ti ho mandato sul caminetto — quella in uniforme, non quella sciocca dalla spiaggia.

Tutto il mio amore, Mario
translation:
Dearest Sofia,

I write to you from the desert, which I confess I have grown to love in a strange way. In the early morning, before the heat begins, it is magnificent — the light on the sand, the silence, the distant blue of the sea. Then comes midday and one would rather be anywhere else.

Rommel's headquarters are not far — one sees his staff cars on the coastal road, trailing dust. The men respect him, even though he is German. He fights alongside his men.

We have been told a British offensive is coming. We will be ready. The Ariete Division has never retreated when it could advance, and we do not intend to start.

I think of you and the children every evening. Keep the photograph I sent on the mantle — the one in uniform, not the silly one from the beach.

All my love, Mario
notes: Tenente Colombo was killed at El Alamein on 24 October 1942, the first day of the Second Battle of El Alamein. The Ariete Division held its positions until nearly all its tanks and most of its men had been lost.

sources.ref: Original letter, private family collection, donated to the Museo della Battaglia di El Alamein, 1978.
Playfair, I.S.O. The Mediterranean and Middle East, Vol. IV. HMSO, 1966.
sources.note: British official history covering the El Alamein battles.

sources.ref: Caccia Dominioni, Paolo. Alamein 1933–1962: An Account. George Allen & Unwin, 1966.

subject: From the monastery hill
excerpt: Father, we are in a position that has no name yet on any map. The monastery above us watches everything. We cannot move by day without being seen...
context: The Battle of Monte Cassino (January–May 1944) was one of the bloodiest engagements of the Italian campaign. The Abbey of Monte Cassino stood at the centre of the German Gustav Line. German paratroopers of the 1st Parachute Division — elite soldiers — defended the hill with extraordinary tenacity against four Allied offensives. The abbey was controversially bombed by the Allies on 15 February 1944, an act that created better defensive ruins for the Germans. Cassino did not fall until 18 May 1944.
original_text:
Caro Papà,

Ti scrivo da una posizione che non ha ancora nome su nessuna mappa. Il monastero sopra di noi osserva tutto. Non possiamo muoverci di giorno senza essere visti. I miei compagni e io viviamo come talpe nelle gallerie che abbiamo scavato nel tufo.

I bombardamenti sono costanti. Arrivo a non sentirli più — è uno sfondo, come il vento. Ciò che mi preoccupa sono i silenzi, quando tutto si ferma e si sa che sta arrivando qualcosa.

Non ho paura di morire, Papà. Ho paura di non aver vissuto abbastanza. Ho ventitré anni e ho visto più guerra di quanta qualsiasi uomo dovrebbe vedere.

Manda saluti alla Mamma. Dille che sto bene. Non è esattamente vero, ma è la cosa più gentile.

Vincenzo
translation:
Dear Father,

I write to you from a position that has no name yet on any map. The monastery above us watches everything. My comrades and I live like moles in the tunnels we have dug in the tufa.

The bombardments are constant. I have stopped hearing them — they are a background, like the wind. What worries me are the silences, when everything stops and you know that something is coming.

I am not afraid of dying, Father. I am afraid of not having lived enough. I am twenty-three years old and I have seen more war than any man should see.

Send my greetings to Mother. Tell her I am well. That is not exactly true, but it is the kindest thing.

Vincenzo
notes: Caporale Esposito survived the Battle of Monte Cassino and the subsequent retreat up the Italian peninsula. He was wounded near Bologna in April 1945 and was hospitalised when the war ended.
archive_source: Commissariato Generale Onoranze Caduti in Guerra, Rome

sources.ref: Original letter, Archivio della Famiglia Esposito, Napoli. Donated 1988.

sources.note: Comprehensive account of the four battles.

sources.ref: Hapgood, David and Richardson, David. Monte Cassino. Congdon & Weed, 1984.

subject: The end in Sicily
excerpt: The island is lost. I do not say this defeatistically — it is a military fact. We hold what we can and delay what cannot be stopped. The men deserve better than this...
context: The Allied invasion of Sicily (Operation Husky) began 9–10 July 1943. German and Italian forces resisted but were gradually pushed north. The Germans conducted a highly effective fighting withdrawal across the island, evacuating most of their forces to the Italian mainland across the Strait of Messina by 17 August. Italian resistance was more variable; morale was shattered by years of losses in Russia, North Africa, and Greece. Mussolini was arrested on 25 July 1943 as Sicily fell.
original_text:
Cara Luisa,

L'isola è perduta. Non lo dico in modo disfattista — è un fatto militare. Teniamo ciò che possiamo e ritardiamo ciò che non può essere fermato. Gli uomini meritano di meglio di questo.

La notizia di Mussolini ha colpito il morale in modo strano — non come una catastrofe, ma come un sollievo che nessuno osa esprimere apertamente. Qualcosa è finito. Cosa viene dopo, non so.

I tedeschi si ritirano in modo ordinato. Ci guardano come se fossimo già la storia. Forse lo siamo.

Non so se questa lettera ti raggiungerà prima che le comunicazioni si interrompano. Se non dovessi tornare, sappi che i miei pensieri erano sempre con te.

Ettore
translation:
Dear Luisa,

The island is lost. I do not say this defeatistically — it is a military fact. We hold what we can and delay what cannot be stopped. The men deserve better than this.

The news about Mussolini struck morale in a strange way — not like a catastrophe, but like a relief that no one dares express openly. Something has ended. What comes next, I do not know.

The Germans are withdrawing in an orderly fashion. They look at us as if we are already history. Perhaps we are.

I do not know if this letter will reach you before communications break down. If I do not return, know that my thoughts were always with you.

Ettore
notes: Tenente Colonnello Berardi was captured by American forces at Messina on 16 August 1943. He was repatriated in 1946 and died in Milan in 1971.
archive_source: Archivio Storico dello Stato Maggiore dell'Esercito, Rome

sources.ref: Original letter, Archivio Storico dello Stato Maggiore dell'Esercito, Roma, F.1 b.22.
D'Este, Carlo. Bitter Victory: The Battle for Sicily, July–August 1943. Collins, 1988.
Mitcham, Samuel W. and von Stauffenberg, Friedrich. The Battle of Sicily. Orion Books, 1991.
